content.text: Now let's check that `Str` has an `as_str`-like accessor and that this compiles.

input.command: source "$HOME/.cargo/env" 2>/dev/null; which cargo || ls ~/.cargo/bin 2>/dev/null; echo PATH=$PATH

content.text: No Rust toolchain available in this environment. I'll proceed carefully by manually reviewing the code for correctness instead of compiling.
This looks correct — `trim_start_matches`/`trim_end_matches` on `&str` accept a closure `FnMut(char) -> bool`, and `Str::as_str()` returns `&str`. Let's write the diff now.

content.content:
diff --git a/crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs b/crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs
index d90b6f206..1c18388bc 100644
--- a/crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs
+++ b/crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs
@@ -179,9 +179,18 @@ impl Str {
     /// Extracts the first grapheme cluster of the string.
     /// Fails with an error if the string is empty.
     #[func]
-    pub fn first(&self) -> StrResult<Str> {
-        self.0
-            .graphemes(true)
+    pub fn first(
+        &self,
+        /// A set of characters to strip from the start of the string before
+        /// extracting the first grapheme cluster.
+        #[named]
+        strip: Option<Str>,
+    ) -> StrResult<Str> {
+        let text = match &strip {
+            Some(strip) => self.0.trim_start_matches(|c| strip.as_str().contains(c)),
+            None => self.0.as_str(),
+        };
+        text.graphemes(true)
             .next()
             .map(Into::into)
             .ok_or_else(string_is_empty)
@@ -190,9 +199,18 @@ impl Str {
     /// Extracts the last grapheme cluster of the string.
     /// Fails with an error if the string is empty.
     #[func]
-    pub fn last(&self) -> StrResult<Str> {
-        self.0
-            .graphemes(true)
+    pub fn last(
+        &self,
+        /// A set of characters to strip from the end of the string before
+        /// extracting the last grapheme cluster.
+        #[named]
+        strip: Option<Str>,
+    ) -> StrResult<Str> {
+        let text = match &strip {
+            Some(strip) => self.0.trim_end_matches(|c| strip.as_str().contains(c)),
+            None => self.0.as_str(),
+        };
+        text.graphemes(true)
             .next_back()
             .map(Into::into)
             .ok_or_else(string_is_empty)

content.text: The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`. It adds an optional `#[named] strip: Option<Str>` parameter to both `str.first()` and `str.last()` in `crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs`, trimming matching characters from the start/end respectively via `trim_start_matches`/`trim_end_matches` before grapheme extraction, and reuses the existing `string_is_empty` error when the trimmed string is empty. Behavior is unchanged when `strip` is omitted. No Rust toolchain was available in this sandbox to compile-check, but the implementation uses standard `&str` methods (`trim_start_matches`/`trim_end_matches` with a closure) and the existing `Str::as_str()` accessor, consistent with patterns already used elsewhere in the file.

result: The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`. It adds an optional `#[named] strip: Option<Str>` parameter to both `str.first()` and `str.last()` in `crates/typst/src/foundations/str.rs`, trimming matching characters from the start/end respectively via `trim_start_matches`/`trim_end_matches` before grapheme extraction, and reuses the existing `string_is_empty` error when the trimmed string is empty. Behavior is unchanged when `strip` is omitted. No Rust toolchain was available in this sandbox to compile-check, but the implementation uses standard `&str` methods (`trim_start_matches`/`trim_end_matches` with a closure) and the existing `Str::as_str()` accessor, consistent with patterns already used elsewhere in the file.
